description: The Turla Kazuar v3 loader campaign begins with the execution of a VBScript file named 8RWRLT.vbs. This script is clean and unobfuscated, suggesting it was deployed on a system that the attacker already had access to, possibly via prior compromise. The VBScript orchestrates the creation of multiple directories under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\HP\Printer\Driver and downloads several files from a remote server. It also executes the downloaded legitimate HP printer installer and sets up persistence. The use of VBScript as the initial execution vector allows the threat actor to leverage Windows-native scripting capabilities for file system manipulation, network communication, and process execution, all while avoiding immediate detection due to the script's benign appearance.
name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: VBScript

description: Persistence in this campaign is achieved by creating a registry Run entry under HKEY_CURRENT_USER\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run. The entry points to the legitimate signed HP printer driver installer (hpbprndi.exe) that was previously downloaded and placed in the %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\HP\Printer\Driver directory. By leveraging a trusted binary for persistence, the attackers ensure that their loader is executed every time the user logs in, blending malicious activity with legitimate software to evade detection and maintain access across reboots.
name: Scheduled Task/Job: Registry Run Keys / Startup Folder

description: The VBScript downloads multiple files from the attacker's server (185.126.255[.]132) into the newly created directory structure. These files include a legitimate HP printer installer, a malicious loader DLL, and several encrypted payloads. The script uses WinHttp.WinHttpRequest.5.1 and ADODB.Stream to retrieve and save these files locally. This technique enables the attacker to stage all necessary components for further exploitation, leveraging native Windows objects to avoid detection and bypass security controls that may block non-native downloaders.
name: Ingress Tool Transfer

description: To execute the malicious loader (hpbprndiLOC.dll), the script runs the legitimate, signed HP printer driver installer (hpbprndi.exe). This installer is designed to load DLLs from its working directory, allowing the attacker to sideload their malicious DLL under the guise of a trusted process. This technique abuses the trust relationship between the signed executable and the operating system, enabling privilege escalation and stealthy execution of malicious code without triggering standard security warnings.
name: DLL Side-Loading

description: The native loader DLL (hpbprndiLOC.dll) contains heavily obfuscated code, including fake API calls, junk control flow statements, and encrypted strings using a XOR-based algorithm. This obfuscation is designed to hinder static and dynamic analysis, increase the time required for reverse engineering, and evade signature-based detection by security solutions. By embedding real code between layers of junk logic and encrypting critical strings, the attackers significantly complicate efforts to understand and mitigate their malware.
name: Obfuscated Files or Information

description: The VBScript collects detailed system information from the victim machine, including operating system name and version, computer uptime, processor architecture, computer and user name, user domain, and a list of running processes. This information is gathered using WMI queries and environment variables, providing the attacker with a comprehensive profile of the infected host. Such profiling enables tailored follow-up actions and helps the attacker assess the value and suitability of the compromised system for further exploitation.
name: System Information Discovery

description: The script enumerates all files and folders within the newly created %LOCALAPPDATA%\Programs\HP\Printer\Driver directory, including their creation and last modified dates. This information is collected using the Scripting.FileSystemObject and is likely used to verify the successful deployment of malicious components, as well as to provide the attacker with an inventory of local assets for further manipulation or exfiltration.
name: File and Directory Discovery

description: After collecting system and directory information, the VBScript sends the data to the attacker's server via an HTTP POST request to https://185.126.255[.]132/requestor.php. The exfiltrated data includes OS details, user and domain information, process list, and file inventory. By using standard HTTP POST requests with custom headers, the attackers blend their exfiltration traffic with legitimate web traffic, reducing the likelihood of detection by network monitoring solutions.
name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

description: The Turla developers employ a control flow redirection trick within the native loader DLL to execute malicious code in the middle of a legitimate function. This form of process injection manipulates the execution flow of the host process, allowing the injected code to run stealthily within the context of a trusted binary. Such techniques are used to evade runtime detection and complicate forensic analysis, as the malicious actions are interleaved with legitimate process activity.
name: Process Injection: Control Flow Hijacking

description: In this campaign, the upgraded Kazuar v3 loader leverages the Component Object Model (COM) to perform patchless bypasses of Event Tracing for Windows (ETW) and the Antimalware Scan Interface (AMSI). By abusing COM interfaces rather than patching system DLLs directly, the loader disables or evades these security mechanisms without altering code on disk, making detection and remediation significantly more difficult. This advanced evasion technique is specific to Turla’s implementation and demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of Windows internals.
name: Patchless ETW and AMSI Bypass via COM Abuse

description: Kazuar is a sophisticated backdoor and remote-access trojan attributed to the Turla group. In this attack, its v3 loader is deployed via DLL sideloading and is used to establish persistence, evade security solutions, and enable remote control of the infected system.
name: Kazuar

description: Gamaredon is a threat group known for deploying backdoors and remote-access trojans, often in collaboration with Turla. The article references a campaign involving both Gamaredon and Turla, implying Gamaredon malware may be present in the infection chain.
name: Gamaredon

description: Turla is explicitly mentioned as the developer and operator of the Kazuar v3 loader and backdoor analyzed in this article. The group is responsible for deploying sophisticated malware using advanced evasion and persistence techniques as part of this campaign.
goals: ["Deploy Kazuar v3 backdoor", "Evade security solutions", "Establish persistence", "Collect victim system information"]
name: Turla

description: Gamaredon is mentioned in the context of a reported collaboration with Turla, as referenced by ESET. While their direct role in this specific attack is not detailed, they are implicated as part of the broader campaign involving Turla and the Kazuar v3 payloads.
goals: ["Collaborate with Turla in cyber espionage campaigns"]
name: Gamaredon

description: Used as the initial execution script to automate downloading files, creating directories, gathering system information, and launching the malicious payload via legitimate processes.
name: VBScript

description: Abused as a COM object to download malicious payloads and exfiltrate victim data to the attacker's server via HTTP requests.
name: WinHttp.WinHttpRequest.5.1

description: Used to write the downloaded binary data to disk, enabling the attacker to save and execute malicious files on the victim system.
name: ADODB.Stream

description: Employed to expand environment variables, run executables, and create registry entries for persistence.
name: WScript.Shell

description: Used to create directories and enumerate files and folders, facilitating both payload staging and data collection for exfiltration.
name: Scripting.FileSystemObject

description: Abused to collect information about the victim's computer name, user name, and domain for reconnaissance and exfiltration.
name: WScript.Network

description: Leveraged to query operating system details and running processes, aiding in system profiling and data collection for the attacker.
name: WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation)

description: The Windows registry 'Run' key is used to establish persistence by ensuring the malicious payload is executed on system startup.
name: Registry Run Key
